coredata.dc:title: Selective determination of dimethoate via fluorescence resonance energy transfer between carbon dots and a dye-doped molecularly imprinted polymer
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shuhuai
Luo, Jinhui
Yin, Guihao
Xu, Zhi
Le, Yuan
Wu, Xiaofang
Wu, Nancun
Zhang, Qun
coredata.dc:description: A novel molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor was developed for dimethoate determination based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer. First, a doped molecular template polymer was prepared by electropolymerization. During dimethoate detection, a competitive reaction occurred between dimethoate and carbon dot labeled dimethoate. Fluorescence resonance energy transfer between residual carbon dots labeled dimethoate in the sensor and methyl red on the doped molecularly imprinted polymer then occurred, enhancing the fluorescence signal of the sensor. The fluorescence intensity decreased when the carbon dots labeled dimethoate molecules were replaced by dimethoate molecules in the samples. Under optimal conditions, good linear correlation was obtained for dimethoate over the concentration range from 6×10−10 mol/L to 3.4×10−8 mol/L with a detected limit of 1.83×10−11 mol/L. This sensor was used to detect for dimethoate in actual samples, for which recoveries ranging from 95.0% to 106.0% were obtained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecular imprinting
Carbon dots
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Dimethoate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs and CDs labeled dimethoate
Preparation of electrodes modified with MIP and non-molecular imprinted polymer (nMIP)
Effect of eluents and elution time
Effect of buffers, pH and concentration of MR
Optimizing the experimental conditions of masking, incubation, and competition
Reproducibility and stability of the sensor
Detection of dimethoate in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Outline of the procedure followed to construct the MIP sensor and determination of dimethoate.
(A) TEM images of CDs; (B) Particle size distribution of CDs; (C) FT-IR spectra of CDs and CDs-labeled dimethoate; (D) Absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs.
CV of the electropolymerization of o-phenylenediamine (6×10−4 mol/L)-doped MR (1×10−4 mol/L) and dimethoate (2×10−4 mol/L) on the ITO electrodes. Scan rate: 50mV/s.

(A) CV of the MIP and nMIP sensor under different conditions: (a) bare gold electrode; (b) MIP electrode; (c) MIP electrode after template removal; (d) MIP electrode after rebinding; (e) nMIP electrode; (f) nMIP after washing. Scan rate: 50mV/s. (B) EIS characterization of MIP films: (a) bare gold electrode; (b) MIP electrode; (c) MIP electrode after template removal; (d) MIP-electrode after rebinding with dimethoate. (C) XPS survey spectrum for the MIP sensor under various conditions: (a) MIP electrode; (b) MIP electrode after template removal.

(A) Fluorescence and absorption spectra of MIP sensor under different conditions: (a) absorption spectrum of CDs; (b) MIP electrode before incubation; (c) MIP electrode after incubation without MR; (d) MIP electrode after incubation; (e) MIP electrode after completion reaction with 1×10−8 mol/L dimethoate; (f) MIP electrode after completion reaction with 2×10−8 mol/L dimethoate. (B) Effect of MR concentration on the energy transfer between MR and CDs. c MR (a–c): 5×10−5 mol/L, 7.5×10−5 mol/L, 1×10−4 mol/L.
Effect of pH (a) and concentrations of MR (b) on fluorescent intensity.
Effects of incubation reaction time (a) and competition reaction time (b) on fluorescence signal response: 5μg/mL CDs-labeled dimethoate, 1×10−8 mol/L dimethoate.
Fluorescence of the MIP sensor after competition reactions in different concentrations of dimethoate. a→p: (0, 6, 9, 12, 20, 30, 50, 80, 100, 130, 150, 180, 200, 220, 240, 260, 300, 340)×10−10 mol/L.

Fluorescences of the MIP sensor after testing with different herbicides. (a) MIP electrode after incubating; (b) MIP sensor after treatment with a 1×10−8 mol/L dimethoate solution; (c) MIP-sensor after treatment with a 1×10−8 mol/L dimethoate solution containing five other pesticides (banvel, parathion, azodrin, methamidophos and phosphamido).
Results for the detection of dimethoate in vegetable samples by the MIP sensor and HPLC.

head.ce:title: Selective determination of dimethoate via fluorescence resonance energy transfer between carbon dots and a dye-doped molecularly imprinted polymer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hainan Provincial Key Laboratory of Quality and Safety for Tropical Fruits and Vegetables, Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Tropical Products (Haikou) Ministry of Agriculture, Analysis and Test Center, Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences, Haikou 571101, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hainan Provincial Key Laboratory of Quality and Safety for Tropical Fruits and Vegetables, Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Tropical Products (Haikou) Ministry of Agriculture, Analysis and Test Center, Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor was developed for dimethoate determination based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer. First, a doped molecular template polymer was prepared by electropolymerization. During dimethoate detection, a competitive reaction occurred between dimethoate and carbon dot labeled dimethoate. Fluorescence resonance energy transfer between residual carbon dots labeled dimethoate in the sensor and methyl red on the doped molecularly imprinted polymer then occurred, enhancing the fluorescence signal of the sensor. The fluorescence intensity decreased when the carbon dots labeled dimethoate molecules were replaced by dimethoate molecules in the samples. Under optimal conditions, good linear correlation was obtained for dimethoate over the concentration range from 6×10−10 mol/L to 3.4×10−8 mol/L with a detected limit of 1.83×10−11 mol/L. This sensor was used to detect for dimethoate in actual samples, for which recoveries ranging from 95.0% to 106.0% were obtained.

ce:section.ce:para: Dimethoate is a pesticide with moderate toxicity. Improper usage can leave dimethoate residue on plants that may cause harm to human health when consumed [1]. Consequently, many countries have strict restrictions on the acceptable level of dimethoate residue in agricultural products. Several analytical methods have been used to analyze dimethoate, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [2,3], HPLC–mass spectrometry (HPLC–MS) [4,5], and electrochemical sensor [6,7]. HPLC and HPLC–MS have high efficiency, selectivity and sensitivity. However, their application is limited by their high cost and long analysis time. The stability and reproducibility of electrochemical sensors do not currently meet the requirements of trace analysis. Therefore, there is great interest in the developing of a sensitive, selective, inexpensive, and simple method for the detection of dimethoate.

Analytical methods based on fluorescent resonance energy transfer (FRET) [8], a mechanism of energy transfer between fluorescent materials, have been extensively studied for sensing applications because of their potential for rapid, convenient, and accurate measurements [9,10]. Carbon dots (CDs) [11] are a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials that are expected to behave as better FRET donors and acceptors than other organic fluorescent molecules [12,13]. The low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and unique optical properties, such as high stability against photobleaching and tunable excitation and emission wavelengths, are also attractive features of CDs [14]. However, research on FRET using CDs is still in the initial stages and there have been few reports [12,13] concerning the potential applications of such materials.

Molecular imprinting [15] is an effective technique to prepare molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) networks containing recognition sites. Such modified polymers have shown promise for applications in separation [16], food inspection [17], environmental monitoring [18], and biosensors [19]. Research on MIP sensors for pesticide analysis includes development of work on electrochemical sensors [20,21], electrochemical luminescent sensors [22] and fluorescent sensors [23]. While electrochemical sensors have problems with stability and reproducibility, and luminescent sensors require development of chemiluminescent reagents, fluorescent sensors are both easy to develop and can give highly reproducible results. Traditional fluorescent sensors use fluorescent dyes or inorganic quantum dots (such as CdTe or CdS) as probes. These probes may have disadvantages in terms of toxicity, and low sensitivity. Such limitations do not apply to CDs, which show promise in fluorescent sensing. The present study is the first report of a molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor based on CDs operating through a FRET mechanism.

Our molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor was fabricated for selective determination of dimethoate. A molecularly templated polymer was prepared by electropolymerization of methyl red (MR)-doped o-phenylenediamine as a functional monomer and dimethoate as the template. As has been described in the literature [24,25], detection proceeds via four steps: elution, blocking, incubation, and competition (Fig. 1 ). Dimethoate in the sample is detected when it undergoes a competitive binding reaction with CDs-labeled dimethoate in the MIP, affecting its fluorescence. In this study, when the MIP was exposed to a light source, the fluorescence of MR (donor) was quenched, and energy was transferred to the CDs (acceptor). The fluorescence of the CDs was markedly enhanced by efficient FRET. Competition with dimethoate decreased the amount of CDs-labeled dimethoate bound to the MIP, decreasing the intensity of the FRET signal in a manner that was linearly proportional to the concentration of dimethoate in the sample. The sensor showed high sensitivity, wide linear range, low detection limits, was convenient to use, and was successfully used to determine the content of dimethoate in real samples.

Electrochemical measurements were performed with an electrochemical workstation (CHI660D, Beijing Huake Putian Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). The classical three-electrode system consisted of a KCl-saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, a platinum wire electrode as the auxiliary electrode, and an MIP-modified indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode as the working electrode. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu UV–vis 1700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence and time scan spectra were recorded on an F-2500 photospectrometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded by a FT-IR-8400 FT-IR spectrometer (Shimadzu). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of the CDs were acquired using a Tecnai 30F (Philips Electronics N.V., the Netherlands).

Dimethoate, banvel, parathion, azodrin, methamidophos and phosphamidon (Pesticide analytical standards, >99%) were obtained from Shanghai Civi Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), o-phenylenediamine and methyl red were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).
All the reagents used in the experiments were of analytical grade. All aqueous solutions were prepared with double-distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and CDs labeled dimethoate
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized using the one-step hydrothermal method reported in reference [26]. First, sodium citrate (0.15g), NH4HCO3 (1.2g) and ultrapure water (10mL) were sealed in a Teflon-equipped stainless steel autoclave. After hydrothermal treatment at 180°C for 5h, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The CDs were purified via dialysis. The CDs were bonded onto the surface of dimethoate using the cross-linking agents EDC and NHS. CDs solution (2mL) were mixed with dimethoate (1mL, 2×10−4 mol/L), EDC (0.005g) and NHS (0.002g). The resulting mixture was reacted for 3h. After reaction, the product was purified via dialysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of electrodes modified with MIP and non-molecular imprinted polymer (nMIP)
ce:section.ce:para: The ITO electrode was cleaned via ultrasonication for 5min each in acetone, ethanol, and double-distilled water. MIP and nMIP were then prepared directly on an ITO electrodes via electropolymerization. The polymer solution contained 6×10−4 mol/L o-phenylenediamine, 1×10−4 mol/L MR and 2×10−4 mol/L dimethoate. The MIP was formed on an ITO substrate by 30 cycles of cyclic voltammetry (CV) in the polymer solution by sweeping the potential range from 0 to +0.8V at a scan rate of 50mV/s. After electropolymerization, the sensors were washed with methanol for 10min to remove the imprinting molecules. The nMIP sensor was fabricated using the same method but without dimethoate. Following each use, the template molecules (dimethoate) were removed by washing with methanol for 10min.

The MIP sensor was immersed in 10mL of 0.01mol/L PBS solution (pH 7.6) containing 3×10−3 mol/L dimethoate for 15min to mask all the vacant binding cavities in the MIP. The sensor was then incubated in 5mL of 5μg/mL CDs-labeled dimethoate solution for 10min to allow the CDs-labeled dimethoate to replace the unlabeled dimethoate. Finally, the competition of the sensor was assessed in 10mL 0.01mol/L PBS solution (pH 7.6) containing of 6×10−10 mol/L to 3.4×10−8 mol/L dimethoate for 10min.

Electrochemical experiments were performed in 3×10−4 mol/L of K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] solution containing 0.5mol/L of KCl. CV was performed over a potential range of 0 to +0.8V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed over the frequency range of 0.1–100,000Hz, using an alternating voltage of 5mV.
Fluorescence spectra were measured over the wavelength range of 300–700nm at an excitation wavelength of 258nm. The excitation and emission slits of the instrument were set to a width of 3nm.

Vegetable (10g) samples and anhydrous sodium sulfate (5g) were weighed in a 100-mL beaker, then ethyl acetate (25mL) was added. The mixture was homogenized with a blender until smooth. After supersonic stirring and filtration, the filtrate was concentrated by rotary evaporation. The residue was diluted with petroleum ether to a volume of 2mL. After drying the collected liquor under a stream of N2 to evaporate the solvent, the final residue was diluted with ultrapure water to a volume of 10mL.

The optical properties of CDs are affected by particle size. The morphology of the CDs particles is shown in Fig. 2A. The CDs are uniform, mono-disperse and spherical with an average diameter of 1.8nm. Fig. 2B reveals that the particle size distribution of CDs is between 0 and 10nm. The functional groups on the CDs and CDs-labeled dimethoate were identified by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2C). The FT-IR spectrum of the CDs (curve a) exhibits absorption bands consistent with antisymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylate anions (1587 and 1396cm−1), and the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups (3200–3600cm−1). These results reveal that the CDs are functionalized with carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. Compared with the FT-IR spectrum of the CDs, that of the CD-labeled dimethoate also contains vibrational frequencies consistent with the functional groups in dimethoate (curve b), with absorption bands consistent with the vibrations of methyl (2950cm−1) and amide (1564cm−1) groups. These results suggest that the CDs successfully combined with dimethoate. The UV and fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs are presented in Fig. 2D. The absorption maximum of the CDs is at 285nm (curve a), while the fluorescence emission peak is narrow and symmetrical, with maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 350nm (curve b) and 450nm (curve c), respectively.

The surface of the ITO electrode was modified with MIP through electrochemical polymerization. As shown in Fig. 3 , a strong current peak from o-phenylenediamine appears around 0.45V. The height of this peak continuously decreases as the number of cycles increases, indicating that the electrode changes from a conductor to an insulator during electropolymerization. This change results from the formation of a compact non-conducting polymer film on the ITO electrode surface.

Dimethoate exhibits no redox peaks in the potential range of −0.2 to +0.8V. Thus, K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] was selected as a redox probe for the imprinted electrodes and substrate solutions. The MIP-modified film was characterized by CV, EIS and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS).

It has been suggested that electron transfer occurs through the cavities in MIPs [27]. Unblocking this route for electron transport could increase the peak current. As shown in Fig. 4A, the compact nonconducting polymer film that covers the ITO surface results in a markedly smaller the oxidation peak current for the MIP-modified ITO (curve b) than that of bare ITO (curve a). Removing the template molecules causes cavities to form, facilitating charge transport, which contributes to a higher oxidation peak current (curve c). When the cavities become blocked by the binding of CDs-labeled dimethoate or dimethoate, the oxidation peak current decreases (curve d). Elution of the nMIP causes little change in the electrochemistry (curve b and curve e) because this polymer film does not contains template molecules, and therefore lacks the cavities that could facilitate charge transport.

Results from EIS of the MIP are shown in Fig. 4B. The resistance of MIP-modified ITO (curve b) is far greater than that of bare ITO (curve a) because of the insulating behavior of the MIP. Removing the template molecules from the MIP reduces the resistance (curve c). Recombining the template molecules with the MIP electrode increases its resistance to almost its original value (curve d).

The composition of the MIP was characterized by XPS. As shown in Fig. 4C, the XPS spectrum exhibits intense signals corresponding to P (2p) at 136eV, S (2p) at 181eV, C (1s) at 285eV, N (1s) at 399eV and O (1s) at 533eV (curve a). These results indicate that MIP was successfully prepared via electropolymerization, and successfully doped with MR. After the template molecules are removed, the signals from P and S in dimethoate almost disappear, the intensity of the N signal reduces considerably, and the signals corresponding to C and N become slightly weaker (curve a).

For FRET to occur [28,29], two mechanistic criteria must be satisfied. First, the emission spectrum of the donor and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor should overlap. Second, the donor and acceptor must be located within a range (r) of 1–10nm from each other. Fig. 5A depicts the UV absorption spectrum (curve a) of the acceptor (CDs) and the fluorescence emission spectrum (curve b) of the donor (MR), which indeed overlap with each other. According to the Förster energy transfer mechanism [30], the major factor that affects energy transfer efficiency is the distance between donor and acceptor (r). Along with r, the critical energy transfer distance (R 0), and the energy transfer efficiency (E) between donor and acceptor can be calculated as follows:
E =
R 0 6
R 0 6
+
r 6

When the energy transfer efficiency equals to 50%, the homologous critical energy transfer distance is:
R 0 6
= 8.8 ×
10
− 25
K 2
N
− 4
Φ J
where K 2 is the dipole space orientation factor, N represents medium refractive index, Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield of the donor, and J is the spectral overlap integral between the fluorescence emission spectrum of the donor and absorption spectrum of the acceptor. Using these formulas, r was calculated to be 5.21nm, which is less than 10nm. These results indicate that MR and CDs are an appropriate FRET pair.

The fluorescence of the MR-CDs FRET system was then investigated under different conditions, as shown in Fig. 5A. Before incubation, the MIP fluoresces at 312nm (curve b). This is considered to be the emission spectra of MR. After incubation, the fluorescence appears at 450nm (curve c), which is attributed to the emission from the CDs in the absence of MR. When MR and CDs are present on the MIP at the same time, the fluorescence of MR is quenched via energy transfer to the CDs (curve d). During the competition reaction, dimethoate replaces the CDs-labeled dimethoate, reducing the quantity of FRET acceptors on the film and thus lowering the fluorescence of the peak corresponding to the CDs (curves e and f).

To study the MR-CDs FRET system further, the concentration of CDs was fixed, and the concentration of MR in MIP was increased gradually, which gave the fluorescence spectra presented in Fig. 5B. With increasing concentration of MR, the fluorescence from the CDs gradually strengthened, which was attributed to more donors transferring energy to the CDs acceptors. These spectra further illustrate the effective energy transfer in the MR-CDs FRET system [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of eluents and elution time
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve better elution efficiency, the different elution reagents methanol, ethanol and acetic acid were investigated. These reagents are all polar, and can dissolve the template molecules and break the hydrogen bonds between template molecules and MIP. To examine the elution effect of different solvents, the MIP-modified electrodes were characterized by CV after elution. The results indicated that the current was highest when the MIP-modified electrodes were eluted by methanol. Therefore, methanol was chosen as the optimal elution solvent. Further testing indicated that the optimal elution time was 15min, so this period was used as the elution time in subsequent studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of buffers, pH and concentration of MR
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain optimal fluorescence intensity, the effects of different buffers and pH on fluorescence intensity were evaluated. Three buffer solutions 0.01mol/L Tris–HCl buffer solution, borax buffer solution and phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.8), were investigated. The maximal fluorescence intensity was obtained in PBS solution. The effect of the pH of the PBS solution on fluorescence intensity also was investigated in the pH range of 7.4–8.4; the results are shown in Fig. 6 . Curve a reveals that the fluorescence signal increased with pH until it reached a maximum at pH 7.6, so pH 7.6 was selected as the optimum pH. The reasons for this were that the CDs were more stable in PBS than the other two buffers, which promoted FRET. Additionally, the carboxyl groups on CDs may be protonated in acidic solutions, leading to the aggregation of CDs and resulting in fluorescence quenching, so neutral or weak alkaline buffer was selected [26]. If the pH of the buffer was too high, the structure of MR could be damaged. More MR mean more donors transferred energy to CDs. The optimum concentration of CDs after incubation was 1×10−4 mol/L (Fig. 6 curve b). When the concentration of CDs was increased further, the fluorescence intensity remained stable because there were no more MR acceptors to accept the energy from the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the experimental conditions of masking, incubation, and competition
ce:section.ce:para: The masking step was carried out in 10mL of aqueous 3×10−3 mol/L of dimethoate solution. CV was performed every 3min during the experiment. The current decreased gradually and then remained constant after 15min. Thus, 15min was selected as the optimal masking time.

The electrode was incubated in 5mL of a 5μg/mL CDs-labeled dimethoate solution. Fluorescence intensity was measured at 2-min intervals. As shown in Fig. 7 (curve a), the fluorescence intensity increased as the CDs-labeled dimethoate replaced dimethoate in the MIP, and it reached a maximum at an incubation time of 10min. As a result, an incubation time of 10min was used in all of the following assays.

The competition reaction was performed in 1×10−8 mol/L dimethoate solution. The fluorescence response was recorded every 2min. As shown in Fig. 7 (curve b), the intensity decreases as dimethoate replaces CDs-labeled dimethoate in the MIP. The lowest fluorescence intensity was observed after 10min, which was thus selected as the competition time.

The incubated sensor was immersed for 10min in solutions with different concentrations of dimethoate under the optimized conditions, and the fluorescence intensity was measured. The results of this experiment are presented in Fig. 8 . When fewer CDs-labeled dimethoate were bound to the MIP film, the fluorescence intensity of the FRET system decreased. The decrease of fluorescence intensity was linearly proportional to the concentration of dimethoate over the concentration range of 6×10−10 mol/L–340×10−10 mol/L (see inset of Fig. 8). The linear regression for this relationship was ΔI =0.82 c (10−10 mol/L)+6.38, with a coefficient of correlation r =0.9989. The detection limit was 1.83×10−11 mol/L (D.L= KS
b /a, K =3).

Fig. 9 shows that the fluorescence intensity did not change after the MIP sensors were immersed in samples containing five other pesticides (banvel, parathion, azodrin, methamidophos and phosphamidon), whose structures are similar to that of dimethoate. This result proves the high selectivity of our MIP sensor for dimethoate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and stability of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility of the sensor was examined by measuring the fluorescence intensities competed in 5.0×10−9 mol/L dimethoate of five sensors fabricated under the same conditions. A relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.8% was obtained, which indicated good sensor-to-sensor reproducibility.

To ensure its long-term stability, the sensors was dipped in double-distilled water, sealed tightly, and stored at 4°C. The sensor was periodically tested in 5.0×10−9 mol/L dimethoate after competition. The fluorescence was measured at regular intervals. No apparent changes in the fluorescence spectra were observed over the course of 1 week. After 1 month, the fluorescence intensity decreased had 12.1% compared with the initial response. This result illustrates that the sensor has good stability over 1 week of storage, and may still be usable after 1 month.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of dimethoate in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of the sensor to quantify concentrations of dimethoate in real samples was examined. Three kinds of vegetables, Chinese cabbage, broccoli and cucumber, were obtained from a vegetable wholesale market and the sample pretreatment procedure outlined in Section 2.6 was performed. The detection results of our sensor were compared with those of an HPLC method [32]. As shown in Table 1 , the detection results of our sensor were consistent with those of an HPLC method. The recovery of dimethoate was between 95.0% and 106.0%, which indicates the high accuracy of our sensor. These analyses demonstrate that the developed sensor can be used to analyze the amount of dimethoate in real vegetable samples.

In the current work, the unique features of CDs are harnessed in a sensor that uses a FRET to improve detection of dimethoate. A doped molecular template polymer was prepared by electropolymerization that not only act as the recognize polymer, but also as a FRET donor. The sensor showed high sensitivity and high selectivity and illustrates a new strategy for pesticide residue detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support received from the National Key Technology R&D Program of China (No. 2012BAK01B05).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Toxicological properties of the organophosphorus insecticide dimethoate
Determination of residues of trichlorfon and dimethoate on guava using HPLC
Residue determination of dimethoate in leafy vegetables (spinach) using RP-HPLC
Determination of residual pesticides in olive oil by GC–MS and HPLC–MS after extraction by size-exclusion chromatography
Comprehensive evaluation of the clean-up step in QuEChERS procedure for the multi-residue determination of pesticides in different vegetable oils using LC–MS/MS
Recognition of dimethoate carried by bi-layer electrodeposition of silver nanoparticles and imprinted poly-o-phenylenediamine
Manganese porphyrin functionalized graphene and its application on dimethoate electrochemical sensor
Graphene fluorescence resonance energy transfer aptasensor for the thrombin detection
Multiplexed fluorescence resonance energy transfer aptasensor between upconversion nanoparticles and graphene oxide for the simultaneous determination of mycotoxins
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Sensing metal ions with ion selectivity of a crown ether and fluorescence resonance energy transfer between carbon dots and grapheme
Aptamer biosensor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer from upconverting phosphors to carbon nanoparticles for thrombin detection in human plasma
Analytical and bioanalytical applications of carbon dots
The emerging technique of molecular imprinting and its future impact on biotechnology
A new approach to determine salicylic acid in human urine and blood plasma based on negative electrospray ion mobility spectrometry after selective separation using a molecular imprinted polymer
Au (III)-promoted magnetic molecularly imprinted polymer nanospheres for electrochemical determination of streptomycin residues in food
Fluorescent ion-imprinted polymers for selective Cu (II) optosensing
Potentiometric sensors based on surface molecular imprinting: detection of cancer biomarkers and viruses
Molecularly imprinted conducting polymer based electrochemical sensor for detection of atrazine
Using a portable device based on a screen-printed sensor modified with a molecularly imprinted polymer for the determination of the insecticide fenitrothion in forest samples
Molecularly imprinted electrochemical luminescence sensor based on signal amplification for selective determination of trace Gibberellin A3
CdSe quantum dot-encapsulated molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica particles for fluorescent sensing of bisphenol A
Highly sensitive molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor based on the double amplification by an inorganic Prussian blue catalytic polymer and the enzymatic effect of glucose oxidase
Molecularly imprinted sensor based on an enzyme amplifier for ultratrace oxytetracycline determination
Hydrothermal synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from sodium citrate and their use for the detection of mercury ions
Fabrication of the sensitive oxytetracycline molecular imprinted sensor based on competition reaction via GOD-enzymatic amplifier
Remarks on Förster's theory of transfer of excitation energy1
Ionization energy of donor and acceptor impurities in semiconductor nanowires: importance of dielectric confinement
Spectroscopic characterization of effective components anthraquinones in Chinese medicinal herbs binding with serum albumins
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer between quantumn dots
Determination of residues of trichlorfon and dimethoate on guava using HPLC

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shuhuai Li received his master's degree in analytical chemistry from Guilin University of Technology in 2013. His major research covered electrochemical analysis and chemical sensors. He now works in the Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences, mainly engaged in the research of detection techniques and biosensors.

Jinhui Luo received his B.Sc. degree from South China University of Tropical Agriculture in 1988. His major research covered tropical crop cultivation. Since graduation, he has been engaged in tropical produce quality and safety standards research for about 24 years. He currently serves as the director of the Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences.

Guihao Yin graduated from Henan Agricultural University in 1999 with a B.Sc. degree in microbiology. He works as associate researcher at the Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences. He is engaged in research work in the food safety and inspection technology.

Zhi Xu received her master's degree in processing and storage of agriculture from South China Tropical Agricultural University in 2003. Since graduation he has been working as an associate researcher at the Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences.
Yuan Le holds a master's degree in organic chemistry from Hunan University and has spent the majority of his career in the study of food safety and inspection technology.

Xiaofang Wu received her master's degree in pharmaceutical analysis from the Sun Yat-Sen University in 2013. She currently works as probationer in the Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences mainly on food safety and inspection technology.

Nancun Wu majored in analytical chemistry and received a master's degree from Jinan University in 2009. Since graduation from this university, she has been working on a research project on the detection of pesticide residues.

Qun Zhang earned her Ph.D. in pesticide science at Zhejiang University in 2012. Her research subject is the toxicity, physico-chemical properties and application of pesticides. Since graduation, she has worked as a research assistant at the Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences.
